original_abstract:
Quality higher education is a cornerstone of national socio-economic development, with service quality serving as a critical metric for institutional success. This study investigates the persistent challenges facing the Ethiopian higher education sector amidst a period of rapid institutional expansion. Utilizing a qualitative research design, the study integrated data from document analysis, focus group discussions, and interviews. A systematic review was conducted on 89 research papers, supplemented by an extensive evaluation of government policy documents, peer-reviewed articles, doctoral dissertations, and empirical reports addressing expansion and quality standards. 

The findings indicate that despite the implementation of various quality improvement programs and regulatory frameworks, these initiatives have had a negligible impact on the overall quality of the sector. Most Ethiopian higher education institutions continue to struggle with a significant shortage of experienced academic staff and chronic deficiencies in essential infrastructure, including ICT systems, library facilities, laboratory equipment, and adequate classroom space. Furthermore, poor dormitory services remain a major grievance for the student body. The study concludes that the majority of higher education institutions in Ethiopia are failing to deliver satisfactory service quality, potentially undermining student learning outcomes. Based on these systemic gaps, specific policy recommendations are proposed to better align the country’s aggressive expansion strategy with sustainable quality standards.
humanized_abstract:
Quality higher education is an important objective of national socio-economic development and the quality of education provided is measured in terms of service quality. Even though there are lots of challenges that are still been imposed to higher education sector of Ethiopia even though efforts have been made towards the expansion of higher education by establishing new higher learning institutions, therefore, this study is intended to assess the challenges of higher education institutions of Ethiopia in the era of expansion using qualitative study design, which employs both qualitative and quantitative data at the same time, hence in this study qualitative instrument of data collection tools such as documentary analysis, Focus Group Discussion and interview were used. Furthermore, 89 papers were taken from systematic review of literature conducted by searching different online databases as well as government policy documents, peer review articles, doctoral dissertations, reports that has direct or indirect relation to expansion and quality of higher education.

The study reveals that despite the number of quality improvement interventions, standards enhancing regulatory measures put in place, the overall quality of higher education sector in Ethiopia does not have any tangible enhancement. Shortage of qualified faculty members and inadequate infrastructure remain common features in all higher educational institutions in the country; while insufficient ICT infrastructure, libraries, laboratories and adequate classroom spaces as well as poor dormitory services constitute the core problems that students have been complaining about in higher educational institutions. Conclusively, the overall service quality provided by higher educational institutions in Ethiopia remains unsatisfactory which impacts on students learning outcomes and overall quality of higher education in the country. Thus, the studies make certain policy recommendations, which if adhered to, could help the country’s objective of expanding access to higher education in an efficient and sustainable manner.
